blocks.title: Building productive cities for an urbanizing world
blocks.html: From regional urbanization paths to skills, pay, city networks, and slums — Urban suite quality v2.

blocks.title: The world is urbanizing, but growth is concentrated in some regions
blocks.subtitle: Urban and rural population, by region (millions)
blocks.source: World Bank staff estimates based on the United Nations Population Division's World Urbanization Prospects, National definitions. Retrieved from World Development Indicators (SP.RUR.TOTL, SP.URB.TOTL).

scenes.text: Although urbanization is a global phenomenon, it has not unfolded evenly across regions. The stacked view of urban and rural populations shows how the world's total population has grown over time.

Unstacking these two groups reveals how each has changed individually. Over the past 25 years, [emphasis: Europe] [emphasis: and Central Asia] have seen little overall population growth, with some growth in urban populations and a modest decline in rural populations.
In [emphasis: North America, Latin America, and the Caribbean], rural populations have plateaued while cities have expanded steadily.

In [emphasis: East Asia & Pacific], urbanization has been particularly dramatic, with urban populations growing rapidly as rural populations declined. The urban population overtook the rural population in 2008, a shift driven not only by migration or population growth, but also by the reclassification of rural areas and expansion of city boundaries, which absorbed once-rural areas into urban zones.

Meanwhile, in [emphasis: Sub-Saharan Africa], [emphasis: the Middle East, North Africa, Afghanistan, Pakistan], and [emphasis: South Asia], both rural and urban populations have grown. In Sub-Saharan Africa, the Middle East, North Africa, Afghanistan and Pakistan, urban populations have expanded at a much faster pace than their rural counterparts.

blocks.title: The number of cities worldwide is projected to nearly double between 2000 and 2050
blocks.subtitle: Number of cities by population size
blocks.source: United Nations, Department of Economic and Social Affairs, Population Division (2025). World Urbanization Prospects.

scenes.text: In 2000, there were just over 9,000 cities in the world.

By 2025, the world had over 12,000 cities. Small and very small cities (50,000–1 million residents) drove some of this growth, expanding from roughly 8,700 to 11,600. Meanwhile, megacities, those exceeding 10 million people, also grew significantly, from 20 in 2000 to 33 in 2025.
But this growth in megacities is expected to slow down over the next 25 years, with projections suggesting that there will only be four more megacities by 2050, bringing the total to 37.
Instead, urban growth will be driven by an increase in the number of small and very small cities, nearing 15,000 by 2050.

blocks.title: Urbanization is associated with economic growth
blocks.source: World Bank staff estimates based on the United Nations Population Division's World Urbanization Prospects, National definitions. Country official statistics, National Statistical Organizations and/or Central Banks; National Accounts data files, Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD). Retrieved from World Development Indicators (SP.URB.TOTL, NY.GDP.PCAP.CD).

scenes.text: Urbanization is associated with higher income levels across economies.
Among lower-income economies, the relationship still holds with wide variation.
Highly urbanized economies span a range of income levels.

blocks.title: Urban areas offer jobs of a variety of skills
blocks.subtitle: Specialization of jobs by skill level across rural and urban areas, latest year available (2008 - 2025)
blocks.source: International Labour Organization. (2025). ILOSTAT Rural and Urban Labour Markets (RURBAN), EMP_TEMP_SEX_OCU_GEO_NB_A.

scenes.text: Each [emphasis: dot represents a country or an economy]. If a job is more specialized than employment overall, the indicator is above 1 for that area. When the value is closer to 1, the job is distributed much like other jobs. Values below 1 suggest that jobs are relatively less specialized. This is reported for urban and rural areas and for three skill levels in each country.

Let’s take [emphasis: Indonesia] as an example, where the specialization value for low-skill jobs in rural areas is 1.07 and in urban areas is 0.95. This means low-skill jobs, compared to all jobs, are 7 percent more common in rural areas and 5 percent less common in urban areas.
Similarly, medium-skill jobs are only 5 percent more common in rural areas and 4 percent less common in urban areas.
High-skill jobs have the starkest contrast: they are 30 percent more common in urban areas and 40 percent less common in rural areas.

More generally, low- and medium-skill jobs are roughly in proportion to overall employment in rural areas (with specialization rates around 1.08) and urban areas (with specialization rates around 0.94). By contrast, high-skill jobs are strongly represented in urban areas, where specialization rates are far higher (around 1.3) than in rural areas (around 0.6). This highlights the role of cities as hubs for high-skill work.

blocks.title: Urban areas offer a variety of occupations
blocks.subtitle: Specialization of jobs by occupation across rural and urban areas, latest year available (2009 - 2025)
blocks.source: International Labour Organization. (2025). ILOSTAT Rural and Urban Labour Markets (RURBAN), EMP_TEMP_SEX_OCU_GEO_NB_A.

blocks.title: In most countries, hourly earnings are higher in urban than rural areas
blocks.subtitle: Share of hourly earnings of urban population above rural population[footnote: A score above 0 means urban pay is higher, below 0 means rural pay is higher.]
blocks.source: International Labour Organization. (2025). SDG Labour Market Indicators (ILOSDG), EAR_4HRL_SEX_GEO_CUR_NB_A.

scenes.text: In most economies, [emphasis: hourly earnings are higher in urban areas] than in rural areas.
Focusing on countries where [emphasis: urban workers earn more] than rural workers.
In a smaller set of countries, [emphasis: rural hourly earnings exceed urban].

blocks.title: Built-up area in Addis Ababa has more than tripled in the last five decades
blocks.subtitle: Built-up area (km²) and population of Addis Ababa, 1975–2025
blocks.source: Pesaresi et al. (2024). Advances on the Global Human Settlement Layer by joint assessment of Earth Observation and population survey data. International Journal of Digital Earth, 17(1).

scenes.text: Built-up area in Addis Ababa has more than tripled since the 1970s.
Population growth tracks the expansion of the built-up footprint.

blocks.title: The decline in slum population has slowed in recent years
blocks.source: Urban Indicators Database, UN Human Settlements Programme (UN-Habitat). Retrieved from World Development Indicators (EN.POP.SLUM.UR.ZS).

blocks.title: Globally, the number of people living in slums increased by 25% in just four years
blocks.subtitle: Total number of people living in slums
blocks.source: Urban Indicators Database, UN Human Settlements Programme (UN-Habitat). Retrieved from World Development Indicators (EN.POP.SLUM.UR.ZS).

scenes.text: In [emphasis: 2018], hundreds of millions of people lived in slum conditions.
By [emphasis: 2022], the number had risen sharply — about [emphasis: 25% more] people.
Side by side: the global increase in people living in slums over four years.

blocks.title: Built-up area will expand by more than 50 percent over the next 25 years
blocks.source: Pesaresi et al. 2024. "Advances on the Global Human Settlement Layer by Joint Assessment of Earth Observation and Population Survey Data." , International Journal of Digital Earth 17(1).
